channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.
channel.copyright: Copyright:(C) Mon, 13 Jul 2026 21:28:25 IST Bennett Coleman & Co. Ltd, http://in.indiatimes.com/policyterms/1554651.cms

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Quote of the day by Muhammad Ali, "The Greatest": "I try not to speak about all the charities and people I help, because…" - a timeless lesson reveals that the purest acts of kindness are often the ones no one ever sees
item.description: Muhammad Ali believed true generosity requires no expectation of return. He deliberately avoided publicizing his extensive charitable work and humanitarian efforts. This approach highlighted his commitment to helping others without seeking personal recognition. The article emphasizes that quiet acts of kindness often carry the most significant impact. Ali's perspective remains relevant in today's highly visible world.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: This supersonic jet needed a radiation meter because it flew twice as high as commercial aircraft
item.description: The Concorde jet soared near the edge of space, requiring careful monitoring of radiation levels to ensure passenger safety. In the face of hazardous cosmic storms, pilots were mandated to descend to safer altitudes. While this supersonic aircraft typically flew above the majority of Earth's atmospheric shield, fluctuations in radiation exposure depended on several factors including altitude, geography, and solar activity. During such storms, safety took precedence over speed.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Poland's 300-year-old 'Vampire Cemetery' mystery: The strange reason 100 people were buried with sickles, locks and unusual rituals
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Britain bought 1,525 acres nearly lifeless farmland, now beavers are helping it make money
item.description: A farm in Lincolnshire is evolving into a lucrative wildland endeavor, with beavers playing a pivotal role in this ecological project. The initiative focuses on demonstrating that restoring nature can lead to substantial private income. Current revenue streams are generated through carbon removal and biodiversity credits, with aspirations to draw significant investments for sustainable environmental solutions.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: UK bans Iran Revolutionary Guards, blames IRGC-backed group for attacks on Jews
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: A supernova lit up Earth’s sky in 1054 and was visible in daylight. Hubble now shows its glowing remains are still expanding nearly 1,000 years later
item.description: Astronomers used the Hubble Space Telescope to measure the expanding Crab Nebula. New images compared with older ones show gas filaments moving outwards. This ancient stellar explosion's remnant is still actively changing today. The Crab Pulsar at the center continues to power the nebula's glow. Scientists also identified previously unrecognised filament structures within the nebula.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: How Israel's Mossad tried to recruit Iran's former president Ahmadinejad; what happened next
item.description: Israel's Mossad reportedly sought to recruit Mahmoud Ahmadinejad for regime change. Secret meetings and financial aid were allegedly provided to the former Iranian president. An attempted extraction from Tehran failed after Ahmadinejad grew dissatisfied with the plan. Iranian authorities later placed Ahmadinejad under house arrest after uncovering his alleged contacts. No official confirmations have been issued by either involved nation.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: H-1B techie, stuck in India for 18 months, sues officials for delay in visa; court dismisses, says it's not unreasonable
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: MacKenzie Scott is still worth $33 billion after giving away $10 million a day for seven years
item.description: MacKenzie Scott, a prominent philanthropist, skillfully balances her substantial wealth with a commitment to giving away billions each year. Having endured financial struggles in her youth through low-wage jobs, her life changed dramatically post-divorce from Jeff Bezos when she gained valuable Amazon shares. Scott's unique approach involves providing unrestricted grants to organizations, a strategy that, while criticized by some tech moguls, far surpasses their charitable efforts.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Archaeologists uncover 3,000-year-old tomb near Egypt's Luxor; inscriptions suggest it belonged to a man named Paser from the Ramesside period
item.description: A 3,000-year-old tomb near Luxor has been discovered by archaeologists. This tomb is believed to belong to a senior official named Paser. Inscriptions within the tomb reveal his prestigious titles and administrative roles. The structure offers insights into ancient burial customs and beliefs about the afterlife. Further exploration promises more details about Paser and ancient Thebes.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Scottish man dies after two 999 calls fail to send ambulance
item.description: Brian Hurton tragically passed away after making two emergency calls that went completely unanswered. During his desperate wait for assistance, he was instructed to remain on the line, battling for breath. Shockingly, the ambulance didn't arrive until over three hours after his initial call. Paramedics found him dead upon their eventual arrival, prompting an investigation that uncovered flaws in the call response process and highlighted a failure to prioritize appropriately.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: India excavated a 28 km artificial lake in the Thar Desert to line it with a plastic sheet: Aims to supply water to 5 million people in Jaisalmer and Barmer
item.description: In a groundbreaking initiative, Rajasthan has constructed an expansive artificial lake designed to augment water supply, benefitting around five million residents in Jaisalmer and Barmer. Lined with plastic to mitigate evaporation, this reservoir aims to maintain water supply during canal repairs. The grand opening of this essential water project is anticipated for July 2026.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Could the 'Hobbit' human species still exist? New theory revives debate over Homo floresiensis
item.description: A strange theory suggests a human-like species, Homo floresiensis, may still inhabit Flores Island caves. These small-bodied hominins, known as Hobbits, were discovered in 2004 and puzzled scientists. Fossils indicate they lived recently, sharing the planet with early modern humans. While local knowledge hints at survival, scientists require physical evidence for confirmation. Most experts remain skeptical due to a lack of verifiable proof for living Hobbits.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: She moved from Los Angeles to San Francisco for an AI job. The biggest difference wasn't the technology
item.description: A journalist moved from Los Angeles to San Francisco for an AI startup role. She found a work culture focused on building products and new technology. Her journalism skills proved valuable in explaining complex AI concepts to a wider audience. While missing her home, she embraced this significant technological shift. She is grateful for the unexpected opportunity which changed her career path.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'Get ready for sudden death': Iran media's Lindsey Graham reference in warning to Trump, Netanyahu
item.description: Iranian newspapers featured Senator Lindsey Graham's death on their front pages. Some publications issued veiled warnings directed at the US and its leaders. One newspaper described Graham as the "Merchant of Death" following his sudden illness. State media celebrated his passing, calling him "sent to hell." These remarks fueled speculation among some supporters of President Trump's movement.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Every autumn, an ordinary Oregon forest suddenly starts smiling after foresters in 2011 planted western larch trees in a secret pattern among thousands of Douglas firs
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: South Korean sailor found dead near border after North Korea ignored search request
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Mitch McConnell issues proof he's alive in a photo from hospital, Internet says release a video to prove photo is not AI-generated
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: From 'Islamic Republic of Japan' to 'Leon' Musk: List of Trump's latest verbal mix-ups
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Snoop Dogg: ‘You can take your boy out the hood but you can't take the hood out the homie’
item.description: The influence of our surroundings extends far beyond mere memories, deeply embedding in our character and daily behaviors. Our formative settings mold not only our speech and desires but also our fundamental instincts. Even as we advance in life, these early lessons remain pivotal, guiding our perceptions and interactions. This ongoing relationship underscores the importance of integrating past experiences as we navigate new paths in life.
